questions.question: Explain the role of the 'Previous Block Hash' field in a Bitcoin block header. (2-3 marks)
expectedPoints: It is a 256-bit number that represents the cryptographic hash of the previous block's header.
It creates the chain of blocks by pointing to the immediate predecessor.
This linkage makes the blockchain tamper-evident, as altering a block would invalidate all hashes in subsequent blocks.

questions.question: Describe the complete lifecycle of a Bitcoin transaction, from initiation to confirmation, including the roles of users, nodes, and miners. (5-10 marks)
expectedPoints: A user creates and signs a transaction with their private key, authorizing the transfer of funds from their UTXOs.
The transaction is broadcast to the peer-to-peer network.
Network nodes (miners and full nodes) validate the transaction (checking signatures, no double-spend).
Valid transactions are collected by miners into a memory pool (mempool).
Miners select transactions from the mempool to include in a new candidate block.
Miners perform Proof-of-Work by hashing the block header until a value below the target is found.
The first miner to find a valid hash broadcasts the new block to the network.
Other nodes verify the block's validity and the Proof-of-Work, then add it to their local chain.
The transaction receives its first confirmation. Subsequent blocks provide further confirmations, increasing its security.
